clinicalStem: A 6-year-old immunocompromised boy receiving chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia is brought to the clinic after his mother reports that he attended a birthday party 48 hours ago where another child was later confirmed to have active varicella. The patient has no prior history of chickenpox and was never vaccinated due to his immunosuppressed state. He currently appears well with no rash or fever. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Administer live attenuated varicella vaccine now
Administer varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) as soon as possible
Start oral acyclovir prophylaxis and observe
Reassure the mother and monitor for symptom development
Obtain varicella IgG serology and defer treatment pending results
explanation: In immunocompromised children without prior immunity who are exposed to varicella, varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) provides passive immunization and must be given as soon as possible, ideally within 96 hours of exposure. Live attenuated varicella vaccine is contraindicated in immunocompromised patients. Waiting for serology results would waste critical time during which the window for effective prophylaxis closes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Live varicella vaccine is a live attenuated virus and is absolutely contraindicated in immunocompromised patients — it could cause disseminated varicella disease.
Correct. VZIG is the standard of care for post-exposure prophylaxis in immunocompromised non-immune children; it must be administered within 96 hours of exposure.
Oral acyclovir may be used in some settings but is not the first-line post-exposure prophylaxis for immunocompromised patients; VZIG is preferred.
Observation is inappropriate for an immunocompromised child — varicella can be life-threatening in this population and prophylaxis must not be withheld.
Serological testing takes time and would likely delay prophylaxis beyond the 96-hour window; in a known non-immune immunocompromised child, VZIG should be given empirically.
clinicalPearl: VZIG is the preferred post-exposure prophylaxis for varicella in immunocompromised, non-immune children; it must be given within 96 hours of exposure.
keyClue: Immunocompromised child, no prior varicella immunity, 48-hour post-exposure window.
commonExamTrap: Choosing oral acyclovir instead of VZIG, or attempting to vaccinate an immunocompromised patient.
missedConcept: Live vaccines are contraindicated in immunocompromised patients; passive immunization with VZIG is the correct prophylactic approach.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old girl with a 2-year history of asthma presents for a routine follow-up. Her mother reports that she uses her salbutamol (albuterol) reliever inhaler 3–4 times per week, wakes at night with cough and wheeze twice monthly, and misses school occasionally due to symptoms. Daytime symptoms occur most days. Her spirometry shows FEV1 78% predicted with significant reversibility after bronchodilator. She is currently using salbutamol as needed only. Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in her management?
options: Add a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) to her current regimen
Start low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) as regular daily controller therapy
Prescribe oral montelukast as monotherapy
Increase salbutamol dose to 4 puffs per episode and continue as needed
Start high-dose inhaled corticosteroid immediately given her spirometry result
explanation: This child has moderate persistent asthma (daytime symptoms most days, night wakings twice monthly, SABA use >2 days/week), which indicates she requires a daily controller medication. The first-line step-up for children not on a controller is a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS), which is the most effective long-term controller therapy for persistent asthma. LABAs should never be added without an ICS, and high-dose ICS is not warranted at this stage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: LABAs must never be used as monotherapy or added without an ICS in children — they increase the risk of severe asthma exacerbations when used alone.
Correct. Low-dose ICS is the first-line daily controller for persistent asthma in children; it reduces airway inflammation and exacerbation frequency.
Montelukast (leukotriene receptor antagonist) is less effective than ICS as monotherapy for persistent asthma; it may be used as an add-on or in patients who cannot tolerate ICS.
Increasing SABA dose addresses bronchospasm symptomatically but does not treat underlying airway inflammation; this is not appropriate step-up therapy.
High-dose ICS carries risks of systemic side effects (growth suppression, adrenal suppression) and is reserved for severe persistent asthma uncontrolled on medium-dose ICS plus add-on therapy.
clinicalPearl: Persistent asthma (SABA use >2 days/week, frequent symptoms) requires a daily inhaled corticosteroid as the cornerstone of controller therapy.
keyClue: Salbutamol use 3–4 times/week, daytime symptoms most days — pattern consistent with moderate persistent asthma requiring a controller.
commonExamTrap: Adding a LABA without an ICS, or continuing SABA monotherapy despite a persistent pattern.
missedConcept: Frequency of SABA use determines asthma classification; persistent asthma always requires a daily ICS before adding other controllers.

clinicalStem: A 3-week-old neonate is admitted with a 5-day history of coughing paroxysms followed by a characteristic inspiratory whoop and post-tussive vomiting. Nasopharyngeal PCR confirms Bordetella pertussis. The infant lives at home with both parents (unvaccinated) and a 14-year-old sibling who received their last dTaP booster at age 11. Which of the following is the most appropriate preventive strategy for the household contacts?
options: Recommend vaccination of parents with dTaP only; no antibiotic prophylaxis needed
Administer oral azithromycin to all household contacts regardless of vaccination status
Administer oral amoxicillin to the parents and observe the sibling
Isolate the infant only; household contacts do not require treatment
Recommend booster dTaP for parents and erythromycin for the sibling only
explanation: For household contacts of a confirmed pertussis case, post-exposure chemoprophylaxis with a macrolide antibiotic (azithromycin is preferred for its once-daily dosing and tolerability) is recommended for ALL household contacts regardless of their vaccination status, since vaccination does not reliably prevent infection or transmission once exposure has occurred. This is especially critical given the vulnerable neonate in the household. Vaccination should also be updated, but antibiotics cannot be deferred.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vaccination alone is insufficient post-exposure prophylaxis; contacts require macrolide chemoprophylaxis to eliminate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary spread.
Correct. Azithromycin chemoprophylaxis for all household contacts is standard post-exposure management for pertussis, protecting the vulnerable infant from re-exposure.
Amoxicillin (beta-lactam) is NOT effective against Bordetella pertussis; macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin) are the antibiotics of choice.
Household contacts are the primary source of pertussis transmission to young infants; isolating only the infant without treating contacts will not prevent ongoing exposure.
Azithromycin is preferred over erythromycin due to better tolerability and simpler dosing; all household contacts including the sibling need prophylaxis regardless of vaccination history.
clinicalPearl: All household contacts of pertussis cases should receive macrolide (azithromycin) chemoprophylaxis regardless of vaccination status — vaccination reduces disease severity but does not eliminate carriage.
keyClue: Confirmed pertussis in a neonate with unvaccinated household contacts — chemoprophylaxis for all contacts is mandatory.
commonExamTrap: Choosing amoxicillin instead of a macrolide, or assuming vaccination negates the need for antibiotic prophylaxis.
missedConcept: Beta-lactams are ineffective against Bordetella pertussis; macrolides are the drug class of choice for both treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis.
redFlagReason: Neonatal pertussis carries very high mortality; failure to prophylax household contacts risks lethal re-exposure in a vulnerable neonate.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy presents with a 4-day history of bruising and petechiae over his trunk and lower limbs. His mother reports he had a mild upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks prior. Examination shows widespread petechiae, a few ecchymoses, and no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. CBC: platelets 8 × 10⁹/L, Hb 118 g/L, WBC 7.2 × 10⁹/L with normal differential. Peripheral blood film shows isolated thrombocytopenia with large platelets. He has no active mucosal bleeding. Which pharmacological treatment is most appropriate for this child?
options: Intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy
Oral prednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day for 2–4 weeks
Immediate platelet transfusion to raise platelet count above 50 × 10⁹/L
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 1 g/kg/day for 2 days
Oral tranexamic acid and watchful waiting
explanation: This child has classic acute immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) following a viral illness, with isolated thrombocytopenia and no mucosal or life-threatening bleeding. For children with platelet counts below 20–30 × 10⁹/L and significant cutaneous bleeding, first-line treatment options include oral prednisolone or IVIG. In a child without active mucosal bleeding, oral prednisolone is a well-established and cost-effective first-line pharmacological option. IVIG is reserved for severe or rapidly progressive bleeding or when a very rapid response is needed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV methylprednisolone pulse is used for severe ITP or when oral steroids cannot be administered; it is not the standard first-line option for a child with only cutaneous bleeding.

Correct. Oral prednisolone at 1–2 mg/kg/day is a well-established first-line treatment for childhood ITP with significant thrombocytopenia; it decreases autoantibody production and prolongs platelet survival.
Platelet transfusion is not indicated in ITP unless there is life-threatening bleeding, since transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by the same autoimmune mechanism.

IVIG is effective and produces a faster platelet rise than steroids, but it is generally reserved for severe bleeding, pre-surgical preparation, or failure of steroids; it is not the first-line choice in this stable child.
Tranexamic acid alone is insufficient at platelet counts this low; it may be used as adjunctive therapy for mucosal bleeding but does not address the underlying thrombocytopenia.
clinicalPearl: In childhood ITP without active mucosal bleeding, oral prednisolone is the standard first-line pharmacological treatment; IVIG is preferred when a rapid platelet rise is urgently needed.
keyClue: Post-viral isolated thrombocytopenia with petechiae but no mucosal bleeding — classic acute ITP.
commonExamTrap: Ordering a platelet transfusion, which is contraindicated in ITP unless life-threatening hemorrhage is present.
missedConcept: Platelet transfusions are rapidly destroyed in ITP; treatment targets the immune mechanism rather than providing exogenous platelets.

clinicalStem: During a measles outbreak at a local school, a previously healthy 4-year-old girl who has not received any MMR vaccine is brought to the clinic by her parents. She was exposed to a confirmed measles case at a playgroup 36 hours ago. She is well-appearing with no rash or fever. Her parents are anxious and ask if anything can be done to prevent measles. Which of the following is the most effective intervention at this time?
options: Administer a single dose of MMR vaccine immediately
Give intramuscular immunoglobulin (Ig) 0.5 mL/kg and schedule MMR in 6 months
Start oral ribavirin prophylaxis for 5 days
Isolate at home and monitor; vaccination is not helpful after exposure
Administer MMR and immunoglobulin simultaneously at different sites
explanation: Post-exposure vaccination with MMR within 72 hours of exposure can prevent or attenuate measles disease in susceptible individuals. Since this child was exposed 36 hours ago, there is still time within the 72-hour window to administer the MMR vaccine effectively. Immunoglobulin is an alternative for immunocompromised individuals or infants under 6 months who cannot receive the vaccine, but is not preferred in a healthy, vaccine-eligible child when the 72-hour window is still open.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. MMR vaccine given within 72 hours of measles exposure is effective at preventing or reducing disease severity in immunocompetent children; the 36-hour window makes this optimal.

Immunoglobulin is preferred for immunocompromised patients or infants under 6 months; in a healthy 4-year-old within 72 hours of exposure, MMR vaccine is the preferred intervention. Additionally, Ig must not be given simultaneously with live vaccines as it impairs immunogenicity.
There is no evidence supporting oral ribavirin as post-exposure prophylaxis for measles; it is not a recommended intervention.
Isolation alone is important for infection control but does not protect the child from developing measles; post-exposure vaccination is an effective and evidence-based option.
Simultaneous administration of MMR and immunoglobulin at the same visit is contraindicated because immunoglobulin neutralizes the vaccine's immunogenicity; they cannot be given together.
clinicalPearl: MMR vaccine given within 72 hours of measles exposure can prevent disease in immunocompetent unvaccinated children; immunoglobulin is reserved for those who cannot receive the live vaccine.
keyClue: Unvaccinated child, 36 hours post-exposure — still within the 72-hour window for effective post-exposure MMR vaccination.
commonExamTrap: Combining MMR and immunoglobulin at the same visit — this is incorrect as Ig inactivates the live vaccine response.
missedConcept: Post-exposure MMR vaccination is effective only within 72 hours; beyond that, intramuscular immunoglobulin extends protection up to 6 days post-exposure.

clinicalStem: An 8-year-old boy with known asthma is brought to the emergency department with a 2-hour history of worsening breathlessness. On examination: RR 36 breaths/min, SpO2 88% on room air, HR 130 bpm, he is using accessory muscles, unable to speak in full sentences, and has diffuse expiratory wheeze. He received 4 puffs of salbutamol via spacer 20 minutes ago with minimal improvement. Which is the most appropriate immediate next pharmacological intervention?
options: Repeat salbutamol 4 puffs via spacer and add inhaled ipratropium bromide
Start IV aminophylline infusion as first-line escalation
Administer nebulised salbutamol continuously, add nebulised ipratropium, and give systemic corticosteroid (oral prednisolone or IV hydrocortisone)
Give intravenous magnesium sulfate as the single next agent
Intubate and ventilate immediately given the SpO2 of 88%
explanation: This child has acute severe asthma (SpO2 <92%, accessory muscle use, unable to complete sentences, minimal response to initial SABA) requiring escalated treatment. The appropriate immediate pharmacological approach is continuous nebulised salbutamol combined with nebulised ipratropium bromide (anticholinergic, provides additive bronchodilation in acute severe asthma) and systemic corticosteroid. Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94% should also be provided. This triple-therapy approach is the current evidence-based standard for acute severe asthma in children.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Repeating only 4 puffs via spacer is inadequate for acute severe asthma; continuous nebulisation and addition of ipratropium plus systemic steroid are required at this severity level.

IV aminophylline is a second- or third-line agent for asthma that does not respond to initial bronchodilators and steroids; it carries significant toxicity risk and is not used as the first escalation step.

Correct. Continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium bromide, and systemic corticosteroid are the combined pharmacological mainstay for acute severe asthma in children; this approach addresses bronchospasm, secretions, and airway inflammation simultaneously.

IV magnesium sulfate is recommended for severe asthma not responding to first-line bronchodilators and steroids; it should not be given as the sole next agent before adequate bronchodilator and steroid therapy.
Immediate intubation is not warranted for SpO2 of 88% alone with no signs of imminent respiratory failure; medical management should be maximised first and the patient reassessed.
clinicalPearl: In acute severe asthma in children, combine continuous nebulised salbutamol + ipratropium + systemic corticosteroid; ipratropium provides additive bronchodilation beyond SABA alone.
keyClue: SpO2 88%, accessory muscle use, unable to speak in full sentences, poor response to initial SABA — acute severe asthma requiring escalated multi-drug therapy.
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to IV magnesium or aminophylline before giving combined bronchodilator and steroid therapy.
missedConcept: Ipratropium bromide has additive bronchodilator benefit beyond SABA in acute severe asthma; systemic corticosteroids must be given early to reduce airway inflammation.
redFlagReason: Acute severe asthma with SpO2 88% and accessory muscle use represents a life-threatening emergency; delayed or inadequate pharmacological treatment can lead to respiratory failure and death.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic after her father was recently diagnosed with sputum-smear positive pulmonary tuberculosis. The child has no symptoms and no history of prior BCG vaccination or TB treatment. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test (TST) is performed and read at 72 hours showing 14 mm induration. Chest X-ray is normal. She has no clinical signs of disease. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Start full 6-month anti-TB treatment (RHEZ) immediately given the positive TST
Repeat the TST in 8 weeks; treat only if it becomes positive a second time
Diagnose latent TB infection (LTBI) and initiate isoniazid preventive therapy (IPT) for 6–9 months
BCG vaccination now will provide adequate protection; no medication required
Reassure the family; a 14 mm result is within normal variation and requires no action
explanation: This child has latent TB infection (LTBI) — defined by a positive TST (≥10 mm in a recent contact without BCG) with normal chest X-ray and no clinical disease. Standard management is isoniazid preventive therapy (IPT) for 6–9 months, which reduces the risk of progression to active TB by approximately 60–90%. Full 4-drug anti-TB therapy is reserved for active TB disease. BCG cannot protect a child already infected, and the TST result is clearly significant in this high-risk contact.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 4-drug anti-TB regimen (RHEZ) is indicated for active TB disease, not LTBI; this child has a normal chest X-ray and no clinical signs of active disease.
There is no recommendation to repeat TST in contacts with a clearly positive first result; a 14 mm induration in a household TB contact is diagnostic of LTBI and requires treatment.
Correct. A positive TST with normal CXR and no clinical disease in a household contact defines LTBI; isoniazid preventive therapy is evidence-based treatment that prevents progression to active TB.

BCG is ineffective against Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection already established; it provides protection primarily against severe childhood forms (miliary TB, TB meningitis) when given pre-infection.
14 mm induration in an unvaccinated recent household contact of smear-positive TB is a highly significant positive result; ignoring it carries a substantial risk of disease progression.
clinicalPearl: A TST ≥10 mm in an unvaccinated child who is a household contact of smear-positive TB indicates LTBI — treat with isoniazid preventive therapy for 6–9 months to prevent active disease.
keyClue: Unvaccinated child, household contact of smear-positive TB, TST 14 mm, normal CXR — classic LTBI presentation.
commonExamTrap: Starting full 4-drug anti-TB therapy for LTBI, or dismissing the TST result as insignificant.
missedConcept: LTBI requires preventive monotherapy (isoniazid), not the full treatment regimen reserved for active TB disease.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII activity <1%) has experienced his third haemarthrosis in 4 months, each involving his left knee. His parents manage acute bleeds with on-demand factor VIII concentrate at home. His haematologist now recommends transitioning to a prophylactic regimen. Which of the following best describes the rationale and standard approach to factor VIII prophylaxis in this child?
options: Prophylaxis is only indicated after 5 or more joint bleeds; continue on-demand treatment
Regular prophylactic factor VIII infusions 2–3 times per week aim to maintain factor levels above 1% to prevent joint damage
Prophylaxis with low-dose oral tranexamic acid daily is sufficient to prevent haemarthroses
Administer factor VIII only on days of physical activity; no scheduled prophylaxis needed
Switch to desmopressin (DDAVP) infusions as the first-line preventive agent
explanation: In severe haemophilia A, primary prophylaxis with regular factor VIII concentrate infusions (typically 25–40 IU/kg, 2–3 times weekly) is the standard of care aimed at maintaining trough factor VIII levels above 1% (converting the clinical phenotype from severe to moderate). This prevents recurrent haemarthroses and the progressive joint arthropathy that leads to permanent disability. Recurrent bleeds in the same joint (target joint) are a clear indication to escalate to prophylaxis, even before 5 bleeds have accumulated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Waiting for 5 bleeds is outdated and incorrect; recurrent haemarthrosis in the same joint indicates target joint formation and is an indication for prophylaxis — earlier intervention preserves joint function.

Correct. Regular prophylactic factor VIII aims to keep trough levels above 1%, preventing the spontaneous bleeds that characterise severe haemophilia and protecting joint cartilage from haemophilic arthropathy.

Tranexamic acid inhibits fibrinolysis and is a useful adjunct for mucosal bleeding (e.g., dental procedures) but does not replace factor replacement and cannot prevent haemarthroses in severe haemophilia.
Activity-based dosing does not maintain adequate trough factor levels; spontaneous bleeds in severe haemophilia occur without any trauma and require prophylaxis not restricted to activity days.

Desmopressin (DDAVP) transiently raises von Willebrand factor and factor VIII from endogenous stores; it is effective in mild-to-moderate haemophilia A but has no role in severe haemophilia A where factor VIII is essentially absent.
clinicalPearl: Primary prophylaxis with regular factor VIII infusions (2–3×/week) is the standard of care in severe haemophilia A; the goal is to maintain trough factor VIII above 1% to prevent target joint destruction.
keyClue: Severe haemophilia A with recurrent haemarthrosis in the same joint — target joint formation signals the need for prophylaxis.
commonExamTrap: Recommending DDAVP for severe haemophilia A; DDAVP requires endogenous factor VIII stores and is ineffective in severe deficiency.
missedConcept: In severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1%), endogenous stores are essentially absent and DDAVP cannot raise levels sufficiently; exogenous factor concentrate is mandatory.

topic: Asthma trigger identification and allergen avoidance
clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy with mild persistent asthma attends a follow-up clinic. His mother reports that his symptoms are worst on weekday mornings when he first gets up, and he has had two night-time cough episodes per week. Peak flow monitoring shows a diurnal variation of 22%. Skin-prick testing performed previously was positive for house dust mite. He is on low-dose inhaled fluticasone. Which additional preventive measure is most likely to reduce his asthma symptoms?
options: Switch from inhaled corticosteroid to oral montelukast
Implement house dust mite reduction measures (allergen-proof mattress and pillow covers, weekly hot-wash bedding, HEPA vacuum)
Add a long-acting beta-2 agonist to his ICS
Perform allergen immunotherapy injections immediately
Prescribe prophylactic oral antihistamines to reduce airway inflammation
explanation: This child has confirmed house dust mite (HDM) sensitisation and symptoms that are worst on waking (consistent with HDM allergen exposure during sleep). Non-pharmacological environmental control measures — including allergen-impermeable mattress and pillow covers, washing bedding weekly at 60°C, and HEPA vacuum cleaning — reduce allergen load and have been shown to improve asthma control in HDM-sensitised children. This is an important and underused prevention strategy alongside pharmacotherapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Replacing ICS with montelukast would be a step-down in anti-inflammatory therapy for a child with mild persistent asthma and residual symptoms; ICS is superior and should be continued.
Correct. Reducing house dust mite allergen exposure through physical barrier and laundering measures is evidence-based and directly targets the identified trigger in this HDM-sensitised child.
Adding a LABA is premature; the child is only on low-dose ICS and has mild persistent asthma; the priority is addressing the identified environmental trigger before pharmacological escalation.

Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous or sublingual) is an option for HDM-sensitised asthma but requires specialist referral and is not the immediate first preventive measure; environmental control should be implemented first.
Oral antihistamines do not reduce eosinophilic airway inflammation in asthma and are not recommended as asthma controllers; their role is limited to co-existent allergic rhinitis.
clinicalPearl: House dust mite sensitisation causing sleep-related asthma symptoms should prompt allergen avoidance measures (mattress covers, hot laundry) as an integral part of asthma management.
keyClue: Positive skin-prick test to house dust mite + worst symptoms on morning waking = HDM as the primary trigger; bedroom environment must be addressed.
commonExamTrap: Escalating pharmacotherapy without addressing the identified environmental trigger.
missedConcept: Allergen avoidance is a cornerstone of asthma prevention; pharmacotherapy alone is suboptimal if the environmental trigger is not reduced.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl with Down syndrome presents in January with a 30-hour history of sudden-onset high fever (39.6°C), myalgia, headache, and a non-productive cough. Her parents report that an older sibling was diagnosed with influenza A 3 days ago. Rapid influenza antigen test is positive. She has received the current season's influenza vaccine. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure parents that vaccination provides full protection; no antiviral needed
Start oseltamivir (Tamiflu) immediately; Down syndrome is a high-risk condition for severe influenza
Prescribe amantadine as first-line antiviral for influenza A
Withhold antivirals since symptoms began more than 24 hours ago; they are now ineffective
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically to prevent secondary bacterial pneumonia
explanation: Children with Down syndrome are at high risk for severe or complicated influenza due to underlying cardiac defects, immune dysregulation, and anatomical airway differences. Oseltamivir (neuraminidase inhibitor) is indicated for treatment of influenza in high-risk patients and is most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset — this child is at 30 hours, well within the therapeutic window. Vaccination reduces but does not eliminate infection risk, and prior vaccination does not remove the indication for antiviral treatment in high-risk patients with confirmed influenza.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Influenza vaccine efficacy varies by season (typically 40–60%); this child has confirmed influenza despite vaccination and belongs to a high-risk group — antiviral treatment is still indicated.
Correct. Oseltamivir is the first-line antiviral for influenza; Down syndrome is a recognised high-risk condition and this child is within the 48-hour treatment window.

Amantadine is active only against influenza A and widespread resistance has made it largely obsolete; neuraminidase inhibitors (oseltamivir, zanamivir) are now the recommended antivirals for both influenza A and B.
Oseltamivir is most effective within 48 hours but still provides benefit in high-risk patients up to 5 days after symptom onset; at 30 hours, it is clearly within the optimal window.
Empirical antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated influenza without clinical signs of secondary bacterial infection; their use increases antibiotic resistance without clinical benefit.
clinicalPearl: Oseltamivir should be started promptly in children with high-risk conditions (Down syndrome, cardiac disease, immunocompromise) who develop confirmed influenza, regardless of vaccination history.
keyClue: Down syndrome (high-risk condition) + confirmed influenza A + 30 hours into illness = within 48-hour treatment window for oseltamivir.
commonExamTrap: Assuming prior vaccination eliminates the need for antiviral treatment in high-risk children with confirmed influenza.
missedConcept: Amantadine has been replaced by neuraminidase inhibitors due to widespread resistance; oseltamivir covers both influenza A and B.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old boy is admitted to the ICU with suspected meningococcal septicaemia confirmed by blood culture as Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B. Public health authorities identify his 11-year-old sister and both parents as close contacts. None of the household contacts has received the MenB vaccine. Which drug is recommended as chemoprophylaxis for the household contacts to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage?
options: Oral amoxicillin for 5 days
Single dose of intramuscular ceftriaxone
Oral rifampicin twice daily for 2 days
Oral ciprofloxacin single dose (for adults and children over 5 years)
Intravenous penicillin G for 3 days
explanation: The purpose of chemoprophylaxis in meningococcal disease contacts is to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage of Neisseria meningitidis, thereby preventing secondary cases. Oral rifampicin given twice daily for 2 days is the standard first-line chemoprophylaxis regimen for all age groups including children and adults. It achieves high nasopharyngeal mucosal concentrations. Single-dose ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone IM are alternatives, particularly in adults, but rifampicin remains the first-line choice in the UK/international guidelines for household contacts including children.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin does not reliably eradicate N. meningitidis from the nasopharynx; it achieves inadequate mucosal concentrations and is not recommended for chemoprophylaxis.
Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is an alternative chemoprophylaxis option (particularly in pregnancy) but is not the standard first-line choice for routine household contacts; rifampicin is preferred.

Correct. Oral rifampicin (10 mg/kg twice daily for 2 days in children; 600 mg twice daily for adults) is the standard first-line chemoprophylaxis for eradicating meningococcal nasopharyngeal carriage in household contacts.

Single-dose oral ciprofloxacin is an effective alternative for adults and adolescents but is less commonly recommended in young children due to potential cartilage concerns; rifampicin is the preferred first-line agent across all age groups including children.
IV penicillin G treats systemic meningococcal disease but does not eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage — it is inadequate as chemoprophylaxis for contacts.
clinicalPearl: Rifampicin is the first-line chemoprophylaxis for meningococcal disease contacts because it achieves bactericidal concentrations in nasopharyngeal secretions and eradicates carriage — IV penicillin used to treat the index case does not.
keyClue: Household contacts of confirmed meningococcal disease — eradication of nasopharyngeal carriage with rifampicin is essential to prevent secondary cases.
commonExamTrap: Choosing penicillin as prophylaxis — it treats systemic disease but cannot eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage.
missedConcept: The goal of meningococcal chemoprophylaxis is eliminating carriage (rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone), not treating systemic infection.

topic: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding in neonates
clinicalStem: A 4-week-old exclusively breastfed boy is brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset seizures. He was born at term via home birth. His parents declined routine newborn care including vitamin K prophylaxis. CT head reveals intracranial haemorrhage. PT is markedly prolonged, APTT is prolonged, and platelet count is normal. Factor VII and factor IX levels are low; factor V and fibrinogen are normal. Which preventive intervention, had it been given at birth, would most likely have prevented this presentation?
options: Intramuscular iron at birth
Intramuscular vitamin K (phytomenadione) 1 mg at birth
Oral nystatin prophylaxis at birth
Hepatitis B vaccine at birth
Intramuscular vitamin D at birth
explanation: This neonate has late-onset vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB), presenting at 4 weeks with intracranial haemorrhage. The pattern — prolonged PT and APTT with low vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) but normal factor V and fibrinogen (excluding DIC or liver failure) — is diagnostic of VKDB. Exclusively breastfed infants are at highest risk as human breast milk is very low in vitamin K. Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg at birth is the standard universal prophylaxis that reliably prevents VKDB and is recommended for all neonates.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Intramuscular iron is not a routine newborn intervention for all neonates and has no role in preventing VKDB or intracranial haemorrhage.

Correct. IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth is the single most effective preventive intervention for VKDB; it has virtually eliminated this potentially fatal condition in settings where it is routinely given.
Oral nystatin is used for fungal prophylaxis in high-risk neonates; it has no effect on vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors.
Hepatitis B vaccine at birth is important for HBV prevention but has no role in preventing haemorrhagic disease of the newborn.
Vitamin D prophylaxis is important for rickets prevention in breastfed infants but does not affect the coagulation cascade or prevent VKDB.
clinicalPearl: Intracranial haemorrhage in an exclusively breastfed, term neonate who missed vitamin K prophylaxis is late-onset VKDB until proven otherwise; IM vitamin K at birth is universally preventive.
keyClue: Exclusively breastfed, no vitamin K at birth, low vitamin K-dependent factors with normal factor V/fibrinogen — classic late VKDB.
commonExamTrap: Confusing VKDB with DIC — in VKDB, factor V and fibrinogen are preserved (liver function intact); in DIC, these are also consumed.
missedConcept: Human breast milk is very low in vitamin K; without prophylaxis, exclusively breastfed infants face serious risk of late-onset VKDB including fatal intracranial haemorrhage.
redFlagReason: Intracranial haemorrhage in a neonate represents a life-threatening emergency; this case illustrates the catastrophic consequence of omitting universal vitamin K prophylaxis.
